text: In this video, we describe our three techniques to promote erectile preservation during male robotic cystectomy.
The surgeon initiates an intrafacial nerve-sparing cystectomy, focusing on preserving the erectile nerves during the procedure.
The surgeon makes a medial incision in the posterior rectovesical peritoneum to develop a medial space, initiating the technique for erectile preservation during male robotic cystectomy.
The surgeon mobilizes the seminal vesicles and vasa deferentia, similar to the monsurus approach used in prostatectomy, as part of the nerve-sparing cystectomy procedure.
The surgeon controls the pedicle to the seminal vesicles using haemlock clips or metallic clips, securing these structures during the cystectomy procedure.
The surgeon places retraction on the seminal vesicles and incises the fold of Denonvilliers' fascia at the apex.
The surgeon identifies the intrafascial plane during the nerve-sparing cystectomy, revealing the distinct tissue layers within the surgical field.
The surgeon dissects the intrafascial plane distally and laterally, controlling vessels entering the base of the prostate medially, completing the development of the medial space.
The surgeon demonstrates the technique for prostate capsule sparing, visibly avoiding sparing the seminal vesicles during the procedure.
Men are required to undergo PSA and MRI testing prior to the procedure.
The surgeon mobilizes the seminal vesicles and vasa, developing a medial space by dissecting the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon sharply incises the prostatic capsule above the fold of the Denonvilliers fascia, entering a plane between the central zone and the peripheral zone of the prostate.
The peripheral zone of the prostate is visually identified by its distinct yellow appearance.
The surgeon continues dissection in the correct plane between the central and peripheral zones of the prostate, extending the dissection as far distally and laterally as possible.
Occasionally, a thirty degree lens may be used to improve the view.

The surgeon uses a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques to develop the surgical plane between the central and peripheral zones of the prostate, following the incision of the prostatic capsule.
The surgeon attempts to identify the plane between the central and peripheral zones of the prostate, which can be challenging to discern in men with very small prostates.
The surgeon continues the dissection towards the apex, advancing as far as feasible within the visible surgical field.
The surgeon continues to develop the lateral plane, extending it as far as feasibly possible.
The surgeon completes the development of the medial space, preparing for the subsequent cystectomy procedure.
We now describe prostate capsule sparing, but with sparing of the seminal vesicles.
The surgeon develops a medial space above the level of the seminal vesicles and vasa deferentia, continuing the dissection process.
The surgeon incises the prostate capsule at the point where the structures enter the prostate to form the ejaculatory duct.
The surgeon identifies the plane by observing the yellow appearance of the peripheral zone of the prostate.
The surgeon uses a combination of blunt and sharp dissection to identify and separate the peripheral zone posteriorly from the central and transitional zones anteriorly.
During the dissection, the ejaculatory ducts become visible in the midline, confirming the correct plane of dissection.
The surgeon divides the ejaculatory ducts, which are visible in the midline of the surgical field.
The surgeon divides the vasa, creating a visible landmark for subsequent dissection steps.
The surgeon develops the lateral bladder spaces after establishing the medial space using a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques.
The surgeon divides the vasa and continues the dissection towards the endopelvic fascia, revealing the anatomical layers and structures in the pelvic region.
The surgeon joins the peritoneal incisions, leaving a strip of peritoneum over the ureter to serve as a second layer for the ureteric anastomosis.
The surgeon clips and divides the ureter, completing the separation of this structure from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon divides the bladder pedicles using a combination of Hem-o-lok clips and a robotic vessel sealer, ensuring secure hemostasis during the procedure.
The surgeon carefully dissects below the ureter, ensuring the dissection plane remains clear of the bladder to prevent accidental entry.
The surgeon performs dissection above the seminal vesicle and the previously divided vas, carefully avoiding disturbance to the neurovascular bundles.
The surgeon continues the dissection until reaching the base of the prostate, carefully navigating the surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon releases the bladder from its anterior attachments after completing the dissection of the lateral spaces.
Whether we are capsule sparing or nerve sparing, we prefer to perform a high release.
The surgeon retracts the prostate medially, develops a lateral plane, and divides vascular structures high on the prostate.
The surgeon completes the dissection on the contralateral side, mirroring the previous steps to spare the seminal vesicles and minimize disturbance to the neurovascular bundles.
The surgeon defats the anterior prostate, revealing the puboprostatic ligaments, which remain intact.
The surgeon develops the plane distally along the prostate, preparing to focus on the dissection around the dorsal venous complex (DVC).
The surgeon performs a sub-DVC dissection, carefully maneuvering to limit bleeding in the surgical field.
The surgeon continues the apical dissection of the prostate, progressing until the urethra is reached.
The surgeon clips the catheter to prevent urine spillage and then cuts it, ensuring control over the urinary flow.
The surgeon divides any remaining attachments around the surgical site, ensuring all connections are severed.
The surgeon places the specimen into a retrieval bag, preparing it for removal from the surgical field.
The surgeon completes the procedure by over-sewing the dorsal venous complex (DVC), ensuring hemostasis and closure of the surgical site.
The surgical field shows the preserved capsule and seminal vesicles, with clear delineation of these structures following the sparing procedure.
And for intrafascial nerve sparing.
And for capsule sparing alone.
Between two thousand and seventeen and two thousand and twenty two, we performed forty one cases of prostate capsule sparing cystectomy and thirty four cases of nerve sparing cystectomy.
Prostate cancer was identified in only twenty four percent of cases where capsule sparing was performed, the majority of which was Gleason six.
In our experience, men undergoing prostate capsule sparing had superior erectile functional recovery and were less likely to require assistance with erections.
